use_cases: Enterprise AED program deployment across locations
Connected AED response system for faster cardiac arrest response
AED lifecycle management and compliance tracking
Workplace safety and OSHA compliance for cardiac emergencies
Replacing outdated AED fleets with connected, modern devices
pain_points: AED programs are unmanaged — devices expire without anyone knowing
No visibility into AED readiness across distributed locations
Compliance gaps in workplace cardiac emergency preparedness
Legacy AEDs lack connectivity — no alerts, no tracking, no data
Cardiac arrest response times too slow due to AED location confusion
buying_triggers: Workplace cardiac event or near-miss incident
OSHA safety audit or compliance review
New EHS Director or VP of Safety hired
AED fleet approaching end-of-life / mass replacement cycle
Competitor or peer company deploying connected AED programs
Expansion to new facilities requiring AED coverage

accounts.name: Marriott International
accounts.why_fit: 7,500+ properties globally, cardiac events in guest-facing areas, outdated AED fleets across all regions.

accounts.name: Amazon Warehouses
accounts.why_fit: 500+ fulfillment centers, high-density workforce, OSHA pressure on emergency preparedness and response times.

accounts.name: Walmart Inc.
accounts.why_fit: 4,700+ stores, no centralized AED tracking, compliance blind spots across state jurisdictions.

accounts.name: Cleveland Clinic
accounts.why_fit: 170+ facilities, cardiac specialists on staff, legacy AED fleet nearing replacement cycle.

accounts.name: Hyatt Hotels Corporation
accounts.why_fit: 1,300+ hotels, guest safety liability risk, connected AED response critical for brand protection.

accounts.name: General Motors
accounts.why_fit: 48 manufacturing plants, 100,000+ employees, OSHA audits demand cardiac emergency readiness.

accounts.name: University of Michigan
accounts.why_fit: 50,000+ students across 3 campuses, cardiac incident liability, AED visibility gap across locations.

accounts.name: Target Corporation
accounts.why_fit: 1,950+ stores, distributed safety teams, no real-time AED readiness data across portfolio.

accounts.name: Ford Motor Company
accounts.why_fit: 30+ North American plants, aging AED infrastructure, facilities expansion requires coverage planning.

accounts.name: Hilton Worldwide
accounts.why_fit: 6,800+ properties, guest-facing cardiac risk, competitor deployments driving safety upgrades.

triggers.trigger_event: Guest cardiac event at flagship DC property, no AED response time logged.

triggers.why_it_matters: Centralized AED tracking prevents next incident from becoming PR crisis.

first_30_days: Call Amazon Safety Director Monday — OSHA citation creates 48-hour urgency window.
Email Marriott VP EHS Tuesday with incident response protocol audit framework.
LinkedIn message Walmart Loss Prevention Chief Wednesday — 4,700 store liability exposure.
Phone Cleveland Clinic Facilities Director Thursday — fleet replacement cycle starts now.
Call Hyatt VP Safety Friday — competitor deployment threat accelerates decision timeline.
Email GM OSHA Compliance Lead Monday Week 2 — 48-plant inspection risk.
Phone University of Michigan Risk Manager Tuesday — student safety liability angle.
Call Target Loss Prevention VP Wednesday — regional incident response failure documented.
Email Ford Facilities Director Thursday — plant expansion + outdated AED fleet.
LinkedIn Hilton VP Guest Safety Friday — industry-wide scrutiny momentum opportunity.

sequencing.why: Active incidents + OSHA triggers = CFO budget approval within 30 days.

channels.reasoning: Incident-driven buyers need real voice, not email. Safety urgency demands live conversation.

risks: Buying committees stall — escalate to risk/liability counsel, not facilities.
Budget already locked for 2024 — reposition around compliance audit cycle.
Competitor already in conversation — lead with liability quantification, not features.
Safety Director hesitates — connect VP EHS to reference hospital system deployment.
